title: Value Propositions and Strategic Fit
category: Value creation, customer centricity, personas, storytelling
content:
As we move deeper into our Information Systems Honours programme, the fourth week has forced us to confront an uncomfortable truth for anyone studying technology: value is not the technology itself. It is incredibly easy to get caught up in the hype of artificial intelligence, complex algorithms, and sleek user interfaces. We often assume that because a system is technologically advanced, it automatically adds value. However, our lectures on Value Propositions and Strategic Fit dismantled this assumption. When we critically reflect on how value is defined, created, and communicated in digital strategies, we must start by asking one fundamental question: value for whom?. If we are building a system for a corporate giant, value might mean increasing profit margins and speeding up supply chains. But when we shift our focus to the South African informal economy, which is the context for our group projects, the entire definition of what counts as "value" changes radically.

In the informal sector, which is characterised by cash-based operations, crime, and limited infrastructure, value is rarely about cutting-edge disruption . Instead, value is defined by reducing everyday friction and addressing genuine pain points in people's lives . For an informal trader or a local spaza shop owner, a valuable digital strategy is one that reduces their vulnerability and protects them against risk and loss . Furthermore, true value in this societal setting is about increasing a person's dignity and agency, empowering them to shape their own economic futures and ultimately increasing their income . It made me understand that a basic, text-based mobile service that helps a trader secure their daily income is vastly more valuable than a complex application that drains their battery and airtime.

Understanding this shifted definition of value requires a completely different approach to customer centricity. Customer centricity in informal contexts means going far beyond traditional user experience design. It means that the digital strategy must fit the context, not the other way around. To create genuine value, we have to understand the lived experiences of the community and recognise their real, daily constraints . We are forced to design for low bandwidth, limited digital literacy, and, perhaps most importantly, we have to design for trust . Trust is a massive barrier in environments where people are accustomed to being let down by formal systems. If we jump straight to a design solution before properly defining the issue, we will fail to create anything of worth .

This is precisely why our fieldwork and community interviews are so critical. The purpose of these interviews is not just to extract data, but to step into the world of our participants and understand their lived realities . We go into the community to validate whether the problem we identified actually exists on the ground, ensuring that evidence rather than guesswork guides our solutions . During this process, we had to learn how to ask good, open-ended questions that do not lead the participant to a specific technological answer. Asking an informal trader if they use a "digital inventory optimisation system" is useless and alienating. Instead, we have to ask simple, contextual questions like "How do you keep track of your stock?" or "What challenges do you experience in managing your business?". By actively listening and avoiding technical jargon, we allow the community members to voice their hidden constraints and tell us what they actually need .

Once we have gathered these authentic narratives from the field, we face the challenge of communicating this value back into our digital strategies. This is where tools like personas and empathy maps become absolutely vital. Personas humanise the problem, helping us to clearly identify pain points, test our assumptions, and clarify the value we intend to provide . However, a crucial lesson from this week is that these empathy maps must be crafted based on real interview data, not imagined stereotypes. If we create a fictional persona based on what we assume a township trader looks like, our entire value proposition will be flawed. Narratives and context shape what counts as value because they force us to see the world through the user's eyes. When we base our strategies on the real stories of real people, we ensure that our digital solutions are grounded in empathy.

Ultimately, all of this reflective and analytical work culminates in one defining concept: Strategic Fit. Strategic fit asks the hardest question of all: does your proposed digital solution truly align with the complex realities on the ground?. The lecture made it explicitly clear that having no strategic fit means having absolutely no impact. We evaluate this fit across four critical dimensions . First, there is User Reality: does the technology match how people actually work in their daily lives? Second is Economic Viability: is the solution financially sustainable for the community in the long term? Third is Business Capability: can the system actually be implemented with the scarce resources available? Finally, there is the Policy Context: does the solution align with existing regulatory frameworks and national development goals? If a digital strategy fails in any of these areas, it will not survive in the real world.

Reflecting on Week 4, my understanding of my role as an emerging Information Systems professional has deepened significantly. I am learning to critically question the default assumptions about efficiency, progress, and what constitutes a "good" digital system. In the context of South Africa's informal economy, value is not about building the most sophisticated application; it is about building the most appropriate one. It is about deeply respecting the power dynamics and inclusion challenges that exist in our society. Moving forward with my Honours project, I am committed to ensuring that the narratives of the community dictate the technology I propose. I want to build strategies that offer a genuine strategic fit, creating solutions that protect, empower, and uplift the people who need them the most.
